edges.description: Carbohydrate-containing refeeding drives anabolic metabolism and acute intracellular electrolyte shifts.
Refeeding can produce sodium and fluid retention alongside electrolyte shifts.
Refeeding can uncover or worsen thiamine deficiency.
The hallmark hypophosphatemic shift is measured as decreased serum phosphate.
Severe phosphate depletion contributes to cardiorespiratory decompensation.
Severe hypophosphatemia can be part of refeeding-related encephalopathy.
Onset hypophosphatemia is monitored as decreased serum phosphate.
edges.source: Hypophosphatemia at onset of refeeding syndrome

edges.description: Intracellular phosphate shift and anabolic phosphate demand lower serum phosphate.
Intracellular electrolyte shifts can lower serum potassium during refeeding.
Intracellular electrolyte shifts can lower serum magnesium during refeeding.
The rapid post-feeding decline is measured as decreased phosphate.
Sodium and fluid retention manifests clinically as edema.
Fluid retention can worsen the cardiorespiratory complication branch.
Thiamine deficiency is monitored as decreased thiamine availability.
Thiamine deficiency can contribute to Wernicke encephalopathy during refeeding.
Cardiorespiratory complications include cardiac arrhythmia.
Cardiorespiratory complications include systolic heart failure.
Cardiorespiratory complications include respiratory insufficiency.
Refeeding-related neurologic disorders include seizures.
Refeeding-related CNS disturbance can include delirium in critically ill patients.
Abnormal myocardial metabolism was associated with histologic myocardial disarray and fibrosis.
Abnormal myocardial metabolism contributes to the cardiac dysfunction branch.
Decreased phosphate is the diagnostic biochemical readout of the hallmark hypophosphatemic shift in refeeding syndrome.
Serial phosphate measurement monitors the rapid post-refeeding decline, especially after parenteral nutrition initiation.
Decreased serum potassium reports the electrolyte-shift branch that can accompany carbohydrate-driven refeeding.
Decreased serum magnesium reports the electrolyte-shift branch that can accompany carbohydrate-driven refeeding.
Low thiamine availability reports the vitamin-deficiency branch that can contribute to refeeding-associated neurologic complications.

nodes.description: The metabolic response to refeeding drives an acute intracellular shift of electrolytes with increased phosphate demand for tissue anabolism and high- energy phosphate bond formation.

evidence.reference_title: Refeeding syndrome: an important aspect of supportive oncology.
evidence.snippet: The typical patient who experiences RFS has been malnourished for days to weeks and develops hypophosphatemia and, occasionally, hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia when administered a carbohydrate load in the form of glucose-containing fluids, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), tube feedings, or an oral diet.
